title: St. Jude the Apostle — St. Febronia of Nisibis & St. Liborius of Le Mans
primary_saint: St. Jude the Apostle also called Thaddeus the Brother of the Lord, St. Febronia of Nisibis the Virgin-Martyr, and St. Liborius the Bishop of Le Mans the Confessor
narrative:
On the twenty-fifth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the proper feast of the holy Apostle Jude, also called Thaddeus, who received his monthly commemoration earlier in the month and returns now for the full liturgical observance of his feast day. Jude-Thaddeus is the apostle of the great Epistle that bears his name, the brief and passionate letter that is among the most urgent in the New Testament: a letter addressed to the community at large, warning against false teachers who have crept into the fellowship, appealing to the faithful to contend earnestly for the faith once delivered to the saints, and closing with one of the most beautiful doxologies in the entire apostolic literature — the blessing of the one who is able to keep the faithful from falling and to present them faultless before the presence of his glory with exceeding joy. The letter of Jude is the tradition's most concentrated expression of the anxiety of the apostolic generation about the preservation of the deposit of faith, and its author's apostolic authority gives that anxiety its proper weight.

The theology of Jude in the tradition is a theology of the tenacious guardian: Jude's letter is the work of a man who has seen what happens when the community is infiltrated by those who turn the grace of God into licentiousness, who creep in unnoticed and begin to redefine the faith from within, and his response is not sophistication or nuance but clarity and urgency — contend for the faith, remember the words of the apostles, keep yourselves in the love of God. The tradition honours Jude not only as the author of this letter but as the apostle who took the Gospel to Persia and died there, most accounts agreeing that he was martyred by an axe or a club, and whose feast day in the West is celebrated jointly with Simon the Zealot — another of the less prominent apostles, another of the foundations of the holy city that the tradition insists on honouring even when the historical record is thin. Jude is the tradition's patron of the difficult case, the apostle whose intercession is sought in desperate situations, a patronage that has made him among the most popularly invoked of all the apostles in certain traditions.

Saint Febronia of Nisibis is one of the great virgin-martyrs of the Syriac tradition: a young nun of the convent of Nisibis in Mesopotamia who was seized during a persecution, brought before the magistrate Lysimachus, and subjected to a prolonged and terrible torture when she refused both to deny her faith and to become the wife of Lysimachus's nephew Lysimachus the Younger — a double refusal, of apostasy and of marriage, that the tradition reads as the double virginity of the Christian ascetic, virgin both to idols and to the world. Febronia's account is one of the most detailed in the Syriac martyrological tradition, and its detail is not gratuitous but theological: it preserves the witness of a young woman who endured every form of humiliation and pain that the persecutors could devise without yielding on either point, and whose composure through it all was interpreted by those who witnessed it — including Lysimachus the Younger himself, who converted after her death — as the composure of one already living beyond the reach of what was being done to her.

Saint Liborius of Le Mans brings the company of this feast into the Western church of the fourth century: a bishop whose long and peaceful episcopate in Le Mans was marked not by martyrdom or theological controversy but by the steady work of pastoral formation, the building of the church's institutional and spiritual life in a region still in the early stages of Christian formation. Liborius is celebrated in the tradition as a man of extraordinary personal holiness and great pastoral patience, and his particular friendship with Martin of Tours — a friendship between two great bishops of Gaul who never tired of visiting each other despite the distances involved — is the tradition's icon of the episcopal fraternity that was the backbone of the fourth-century Western church. The twenty-fifth day of Sene gathers around the feast of the apostle of the urgent letter the Syriac virgin who refused every compromise and the Gallic bishop who built the Church one pastoral visit at a time. Amen.
